- ~70% agricultural workers (outdoors most of the day): **~3 min/day**

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

| Factor | Estimate | Reasoning |
|--------|----------|-----------|
| **World population** | ~1 billion | Standard demographic estimate for 1800 |
| **Fraction with window access** | ~45% (~450 million) | Europe/East Asia had windowed structures; much of rural Africa, nomadic/tribal populations, and tropical regions had minimal or no framed window openings |
| **Avg. daily gazing time** | ~5 minutes/day | Most people were agricultural laborers outdoors 10–14 hrs; indoor waking time was limited; window gazing = checking weather, brief idle glances, watching the street. No competing indoor media, but also little leisure time |

## Calculation

$$450{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 5 \;\text{min/day} \times 365 \;\text{days/year} = 821{,}250{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-minutes}$$

$$\frac{821{,}250{,}000{,}000}{1{,}440 \;\text{min/day}} \approx 570{,}000{,}000 \;\text{days}$$

## My Point Estimate

$$\boxed{\approx 570{,}000{,}000 \text{ days}}$$

That's roughly **570 million person-days** — or equivalently about 1.56 million person-years — spent collectively looking through windows in the year 1800.

- The vast majority were agricultural laborers, outdoors dawn to dusk (~2–3 min/day)
- Urban workers, craftspeople indoors more (~7 min/day)

$$550{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 4 \;\text{min/day} \times 365 \;\text{days} = 803 \;\text{billion minutes}$$

$$\frac{803{,}000{,}000{,}000}{1{,}440 \;\text{min/day}} \approx 557{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

## My point estimate:

## **550,000,000 days** (~550 million person-days)

In 1800, most of humanity lived in agrarian settings. Glass windows were a relative luxury (common in wealthier European/American homes, rare in much of Asia, Africa, and among the poor). However, many cultures had openable shutters, paper-screen windows (East Asia), or similar openings.

- **~15%** were urban workers, craftspeople, or domestics — more indoor time. Maybe **10 min/day**.
- **~5%** were wealthy, elderly, or infirm — significantly more indoor time. Maybe **20 min/day**.

**Weighted average ≈ 5 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$450{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 5 \;\text{min/day} \times 365 \;\text{days/year} = 821{,}250{,}000{,}000 \;\text{minutes}$$

$$\frac{821{,}250{,}000{,}000}{1{,}440 \;\text{min/day}} \approx 570{,}000{,}000 \;\text{days}$$

## My Point Estimate

$$\boxed{570{,}000{,}000 \text{ days}}$$

That's roughly **570 million person-days** spent looking through a window in 1800.

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

### World Population & Window Access

| Region | Population | % with glass windows | People with access |
|---|---|---|---|
| Western Europe | ~110M | ~65% | ~72M |
| Eastern/Southern Europe | ~90M | ~35% | ~31M |
| Americas (settler pop.) | ~10M | ~55% | ~6M |
| Asia (~600M) | ~600M | ~0.5% | ~3M |
| Middle East/Africa/Other | ~130M | ~1% | ~1.3M |
| **Total** | **~1 billion** | | **~113M** |

*Note: Glass windows were largely a European phenomenon in 1800. Chinese/Japanese architecture used opaque paper screens.*

### Average Daily Window-Gazing Time (~7 min/day)
- Laborers/farmers (outside most of the day): ~5 min/day
- Domestic workers/housewives: ~12 min/day
- Children: ~5 min/day
- Elderly/wealthy/idle: ~15 min/day
- **Population-weighted average: ≈ 7 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$113{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 7 \;\text{min/day} \times 365 \;\text{days} = 289 \;\text{billion minutes}$$

$$= 4.8 \;\text{billion hours} \;= \;201 \;\text{million person-days}$$

## My Point Estimate

$$\boxed{200{,}000{,}000 \text{ person-days}}$$

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Assumptions

| Parameter | Estimate | Reasoning |
|-----------|----------|-----------|
| **World population** | ~1 billion | Standard historical estimate |
| **Fraction with window access** | ~40% (400 million) | Glass windows were common in Europe/East Asia (paper windows in China/Japan), but much of the world lived in basic structures (huts, tents, open-air dwellings) with no real window openings |
| **Average daily gazing time** | ~6 minutes/day | Most people (~70%) were outdoor agricultural workers (≈3 min/day of glancing out); indoor/domestic workers gazed more (~15 min/day); adjusted downward for infants/young children who don't window-gaze |

## Calculation

$$400{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 6 \;\text{min/day} \times 365 \;\text{days} = 876{,}000{,}000{,}000 \;\text{minutes}$$

$$\frac{876{,}000{,}000{,}000}{1{,}440 \;\text{min/day}} \approx 608{,}000{,}000 \;\text{days}$$

## My Point Estimate

$$\boxed{\approx 600{,}000{,}000 \text{ person-days}}$$

That's roughly **600 million days** — equivalent to about 1.6 million years of continuous window-gazing, produced collectively by humanity in the year 1800.

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing Time in 1800

## Key Parameters

### World Population in 1800
~**1 billion** people.

### Fraction with Meaningful Window Access
Not everyone had a window to look "through":
- **Europe (~200M):** Glass windows fairly widespread, even in modest homes → ~60% ≈ 120M
- **China (~300M):** Paper/lattice windows common in many homes → ~30% (partially transparent) ≈ 90M
- **India (~200M):** Fewer glazed openings → ~10% ≈ 20M
- **Americas, Middle East, Japan, others (~300M):** Mixed → ~10% ≈ 30M

**Effective window-access population: ~200 million**

### Average Daily Time Looking Through a Window
Most people in 1800 were agricultural laborers spending the majority of daylight hours outdoors. Indoor time with incidental or deliberate window gazing would include:
- Checking weather: ~1–2 min
- Casual glances during indoor chores: ~2–3 min
- Evening/morning idle moments: ~2 min

**Weighted average (skewed toward outdoor workers): ~7 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$200{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 7 \;\text{min/day} \times 365 \;\text{days} = 511 \times 10^9 \;\text{person-minutes}$$

$$\frac{511 \times 10^9}{1{,}440 \;\text{min/day}} \approx 355{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

---

## My Estimate: **350,000,000 person-days**

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**People with access to see-through windows (glass):**

| Region | Population | % with glass windows | Count |
|---|---|---|---|
| Europe | ~200M | ~50% | 100M |
| China | ~330M | ~5% | 16M |
| Americas | ~30M | ~15% | 4.5M |
| India | ~200M | ~2% | 4M |
| Middle East | ~30M | ~10% | 3M |
| Other Asia | ~100M | ~3% | 3M |
| Africa | ~100M | ~1% | 1M |
| **Total regular access** | | | **~130M** |

I add ~100M occasional viewers (churches, public buildings, visiting others).

**Average daily gazing time:**
Most people in 1800 worked long physical hours. For those with window access, I estimate ~7 minutes/day (checking weather, watching streets/children, idle glances while doing indoor work). Occasional viewers: ~1 min/day equivalent.

## Calculation

Total person-minutes/day: (130M × 7) + (100M × 1) = **1.01 billion**
Per year: 1.01B × 365 = **368.7 billion person-minutes**
Convert to person-days (÷1440):

## **≈ 260,000,000 person-days**

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

### World Population (1800)
Approximately **978 million** (well-established historical estimate).

### Fraction with Window Access (~45%)
| Region | Population | Window Access | People w/ Windows |
|---|---|---|---|
| Europe (~200M) | Glass windows common even in modest homes | ~70% | 140M |
| East Asia (~400M) | Paper/lattice windows widespread | ~50% | 200M |
| South Asia (~200M) | Window openings varied | ~30% | 60M |
| Africa (~100M) | Traditional homes, minimal windows | ~15% | 15M |
| Rest of world (~78M) | Mixed | ~30% | ~25M |
| **Total** | | | **~440 million** |

### Average Daily Window-Gazing (~5 minutes/day)
- **Farmers (majority ~70%):** Outdoors most of the day; brief glances at meals and evenings → ~3–4 min/day
- **Urban/craftspeople (~15%):** Work near windows for light, periodic glances → ~10 min/day
- **Wealthy/elderly/children (~15%):** More indoor leisure → ~8–15 min/day
- **Weighted average ≈ 5 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$440{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 5\;\text{min/day} \times 365\;\text{days} = 803\;\text{billion person-minutes}$$

$$\frac{803{,}000{,}000{,}000}{1{,}440\;\text{min/day}} \approx 557{,}000{,}000\;\text{person-days}$$

---

## My point estimate: **550,000,000 days** (≈ 550 million person-days)

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Assumptions & Reasoning

**World Population (1800):** ~1 billion

**Fraction with access to some form of window:**
| Region | Population | Window Access Rate | People w/ Windows |
|---|---|---|---|
| Europe | ~200M | ~70% | ~140M |
| China/Japan | ~360M | ~45% (paper/lattice windows) | ~160M |
| Indian Subcontinent | ~200M | ~30% | ~60M |
| Americas | ~55M | ~35% | ~19M |
| Middle East/N. Africa | ~30M | ~50% | ~15M |
| Rest of world | ~155M | ~15% | ~23M |
| **Total** | **~1B** | | **~420M** |

After excluding very young children (under ~3): **≈ 400 million effective window-gazers**

**Average daily window-gazing time (~5 min/day):**
- Most people were agricultural laborers, outdoors most of the day
- Indoor time was dominated by domestic work, not leisure
- Windows were often small; gazing was incidental (checking weather, watching livestock/children, brief glances)
- Weighted across farmers (~3–4 min), domestic workers (~8–10 min), urban/wealthy (~10–20 min), children (~3 min)

## Calculation

$$400{,}000{,}000 \text{ people} \times 365 \text{ days} \times 5 \text{ min/day} = 730 \text{ billion minutes}$$

$$\frac{730{,}000{,}000{,}000}{1{,}440 \text{ min/day}} \approx 507{,}000{,}000 \text{ days}$$

## My Point Estimate

$$\boxed{500{,}000{,}000 \text{ days}}$$

That is, roughly **500 million person-days** spent looking through a window in the year 1800.

(Roughly 600 million person-days, i.e., about 1.7 million person-years of cumulative window-gazing across all of humanity in 1800.)

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Assumptions & Reasoning

### World Population
~**1 billion** people in 1800.

### Fraction with Access to a Window
Not everyone had a window to look through:
- **Europe (~200M):** Glass windows common among middle/upper classes; many rural poor used shutters. ~50% → 100M
- **China (~300M):** Paper-screen windows nearly ubiquitous in houses, but hard to truly "look through." ~40% → 120M
- **India (~200M):** Traditional homes often had openings/jharokhas. ~30% → 60M
- **Americas, Africa, rest of world (~300M):** Mix of permanent and impermanent structures. ~20% → 60M

**Total with window access ≈ 350–500 million → I'll use 450 million**

### Average Daily Time Spent Looking Through a Window
Most people (≈70%) worked outdoors (agriculture). Weighted estimate:

| Segment | Share | Min/day |
|---|---|---|
| Outdoor laborers | 70% | 2 |
| Indoor workers/domestic | 15% | 5 |
| Upper class/merchants | 5% | 10 |
| Young children/elderly/sick | 10% | 5 |

**Weighted average ≈ 3 minutes/day**

### Calculation

$$450{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 3 \;\text{min/day} \times 365 \;\text{days} = 492{,}750{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-minutes}$$

$$\frac{492{,}750{,}000{,}000}{1{,}440 \;\text{min/day}} \approx 342{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

Rounding slightly upward to account for seasonal variation (long winters indoors in northern latitudes, rainy seasons, etc.):

## **≈ 400,000,000 days**

*(About 400 million person-days, or roughly 1.1 million person-years.)*

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Assumptions

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**Fraction with meaningful window access (~45%):**
- **Europe (~200M):** ~80% had glass or openable windows → 160M
- **China (~300M):** Paper windows (semi-transparent, limited gazing); maybe 30% could effectively look through → 90M
- **India/South Asia (~200M):** ~25% → 50M
- **Americas (~50M):** ~40% → 20M
- **Middle East/North Africa (~30M):** ~45% → ~14M
- **Sub-Saharan Africa/Other (~120M):** ~10% → ~12M
- **Total with access:** ~450 million

**Average daily gazing time (for those with access): ~3 minutes/day**
- ~90% rural: brief weather/livestock checks → ~2 min/day
- ~10% urban/homebound: street-watching, idle gazing → ~8 min/day
- Weighted: ~2.6 min, rounded to **3 min/day**

## Calculation

$$1{,}000{,}000{,}000 \times 0.45 \times \frac{3 \text{ min}}{1440 \text{ min/day}} \times 365 \text{ days}$$

$$= 1{,}000{,}000{,}000 \times 0.45 \times 0.002083 \times 365 \approx 342{,}000{,}000$$

## My Point Estimate

## **≈ 340,000,000 person-days**

(roughly 340 million days of cumulative window-gazing across all humanity in 1800)

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing Time in 1800

## Key Assumptions & Reasoning

### World Population in 1800
~**1 billion** people (well-established historical estimate).

### Fraction with Access to a Window (broadly defined)
A "window" can be any purposeful opening in a wall (not just glass). Still, many people lived in basic huts, tents, or structures without proper windows.

| South Asia | ~200M | ~15% | ~30M |

| Americas/Other | ~100M | ~20% | ~20M |

Most people (~80%) were outdoor agricultural laborers; evenings were dark (no point gazing). Weighted estimate:

- Men (outdoor workers): ~2 min/day
- Women (more indoor housework): ~5 min/day
- Children: ~3 min/day

**Population-weighted average ≈ 4 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$600{,}000{,}000 \text{ people} \times 4 \text{ min/day} \times 365 \text{ days} = 8.76 \times 10^{11} \text{ person-minutes}$$

$$\frac{8.76 \times 10^{11}}{1{,}440 \text{ min/day}} \approx 608{,}000{,}000 \text{ days}$$

### Sanity check
That's roughly 1 full day per person per year, or ~4 min/day — plausible for a pre-industrial, mostly outdoor-working population.

---

## **My estimate: 600,000,000 days**

| N. American settlers | ~10M | 3 |
| Latin American/Middle Eastern urban | ~10M | 4 |
| Asian with window access | ~50M | 3 |
| Rest of world (open window/no window) | ~730M | ~0.5–1 |

### Reasoning on Duration
Most people in 1800 were outdoor agricultural laborers — very little idle indoor time. Window-gazing was mainly: checking the weather, watching for visitors, brief glances during indoor chores. No electric light meant windows served as light sources, but *deliberate looking through* was brief.

## Calculation

Summing across segments gives roughly **1.5 billion person-minutes per day** worldwide.

$$\boxed{400{,}000{,}000 \text{ days}}$$

That is, roughly **400 million person-days** of window-gazing across all of humanity in 1800.

$$= 550{,}000{,}000 \times 1.267 \approx 700{,}000{,}000$$

## **≈ 700,000,000 days** (700 million days)

~1 billion people in 1800.

- **India (~200M):** Many simple dwellings with minimal or shuttered openings; ~15% → ~30M
- **Americas (~30M):** Mixed frontier/colonial; ~40% → ~12M
- **Middle East, SE Asia, Africa, others (~240M):** Generally low rates → ~25M

**Total with meaningful window access ≈ 350–500 million** → I'll use **~400 million**

### Average Daily Window-Gazing Time
Most people (80%+) were outdoor agricultural workers — minimal gazing. Indoor workers, domestic workers, elderly, and children indoors gaze more:

|---|---|
| Agricultural workers (~70%) | ~2–3 |
| Domestic/indoor workers (~20%) | ~8–10 |
| Elderly/infirm (~10%) | ~12–15 |

**Weighted average ≈ 5 minutes/day** for those with windows.

## Calculation

$$400{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 5\;\text{min/day} \times 365\;\text{days} \div 1{,}440\;\text{min/day} \approx 507{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

## Sanity Check
This implies each of the 1 billion humans averaged ~2 min/day of window-gazing (including those without windows) — about 0.14% of total time. That feels plausible.

---

## **My point estimate: 500,000,000 person-days**

Looking at this more carefully, "looking through a window" is a specific activity—gazing out rather than just being in a room that happens to have windows. Now I'm working through the math on my estimates. answer:
# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

| Factor | Estimate | Reasoning |
|--------|----------|-----------|
| **World population** | ~1 billion | Standard historical estimate for 1800 |
| **Fraction with window access** | ~50% (500 million) | Glass windows were mostly a European/East Asian luxury; but many structures had openings or shuttered windows. Accounting for rural poor, nomads, tropical open dwellings, infants, etc. |
| **Avg. daily gazing time** (for those with access) | ~3.5 minutes/day | Most were farmers/laborers outdoors 10+ hrs/day. Indoor time near windows for light (sewing, reading) yields occasional glances. Wealthier/elderly/housebound people pull the average up; laborers pull it down. No electric light means almost zero nighttime gazing. |

## Calculation

$$500\text{M people} \times 3.5 \;\text{min/day} \times 365 \;\text{days} = 639 \;\text{billion person-minutes}$$

$$\frac{639 \times 10^9 \;\text{minutes}}{1{,}440 \;\text{min/day}} \approx 444 \;\text{million person-days}$$

### Sanity check
This implies ~1.75 min/day averaged over *every* human on Earth — plausible given that vast populations in South Asia, Africa, and rural China had minimal transparent windows or spent nearly all day outdoors.

## My Point Estimate

$$\boxed{\approx 450 \text{ million person-days}}$$

answer:
# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing Time in 1800

## Key Parameter Estimates

### 1. World Population (1800)
≈ **1 billion** people

### 2. Fraction with Access to a Window
Not everyone had glazed windows, but I'll count any framed wall opening (glass, paper, open shutter) one can look *through*:

| Region | Population | Window-access rate | People with windows |
|---|---|---|---|
| Europe | 200M | ~60% | 120M |
| East Asia (China, Japan, Korea) | 360M | ~40% | 144M |
| South Asia | 200M | ~15% | 30M |
| Middle East / N. Africa | 50M | ~30% | 15M |
| Sub-Saharan Africa | 100M | ~5% | 5M |
| Americas | 25M | ~30% | 7.5M |
| Other | 65M | ~15% | 10M |
| **Total** | **1B** | | **~330M** |

Removing infants and others unable to gaze purposefully (~15%): **~280 million effective window-gazers**

### 3. Average Daily Window-Gazing Time
Most people in 1800 were outdoor agricultural laborers — indoor time was limited. Brief glances to check weather, during meals, or in evening add up:
- Farmers: ~4 min/day
- Urban/artisan workers: ~10 min/day
- Wealthy/elderly/housebound: ~20 min/day

**Weighted average ≈ 6 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$280{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 6 \;\text{min/day} \times 365 \;\text{days} = 613{,}200{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-minutes}$$

$$\frac{613{,}200{,}000{,}000}{1{,}440 \;\text{min/day}} \approx 425{,}800{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

---

## My Point Estimate

$$\boxed{~450{,}000{,}000 \text{ person-days}}$$

That is, roughly **450 million days** of cumulative human window-gazing in 1800.

answer:
# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing Time in 1800

## Key Parameters

### World Population in 1800
~**1 billion** people.

### Fraction with "look-through-able" windows
Not everyone had a window they could actually see through:
- **Europe/Russia (~235M):** Most dwellings had glass or open-shutter windows → ~70% → **165M**
- **China (~330M):** Paper-lattice windows were common but mostly opaque; open windows sometimes → ~15% effectively see-through → **50M**
- **India/S. Asia (~200M):** Many simple structures, some with openings → ~15% → **30M**
- **Japan/SE Asia (~65M):** Paper screens, open structures → ~15% → **10M**
- **Americas (~30M):** Mixed; colonial towns had glass, rural/indigenous often didn't → ~25% → **8M**
- **Africa (~100M), Middle East (~40M):** Limited glass; some open windows → ~10% → **14M**

**Total with functional see-through windows: ~280–350 million → I'll use 350 million**

### Average daily window-gazing time (per person with access)
In 1800, ~70%+ were agricultural laborers outdoors most of the day:
| Group | % of window-havers | Avg min/day |
|---|---|---|
| Farmers/laborers | 65% | 3 min |
| Urban workers/artisans | 15% | 7 min |
| Wealthy/leisured | 5% | 20 min |
| Homemakers, elderly, children | 15% | 5 min |

**Weighted average ≈ 5 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$350{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 5 \;\text{min/day} \times 365 \;\text{days} = 638{,}750{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-minutes}$$

$$\frac{638{,}750{,}000{,}000}{1{,}440 \;\text{min/day}} \approx 443{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

---

## My point estimate: **400,000,000 person-days**

(≈ 400 million person-days, or roughly 1.1 million person-*years* of cumulative window-gazing across all humanity in 1800.)

## Key Parameters

### World Population in 1800
Approximately **1 billion** people.

### Fraction with Access to Windows
Not everyone had a transparent or open window to look through:

| Group | Population | % with usable windows | People with windows |
|---|---|---|---|
| Europe | ~200M | ~45% | ~90M |
| East Asia (paper/glass) | ~400M | ~5% | ~20M |
| Americas | ~7M | ~35% | ~2.5M |
| Middle East | ~30M | ~8% | ~2.5M |
| South Asia, Africa, other | ~360M | ~1–2% | ~5M |
| **Total** | | | **~120M** |

Adjusting upward for open/unglazed window openings in warmer climates adds roughly another **80M**, giving about **200 million** people who meaningfully looked through a window.

### Average Daily Window-Gazing Time
Most people were outdoor agricultural workers. Even those with windows:
- Might glance out to check weather, watch for visitors, supervise children, or gaze idly: ~**10 minutes/day** on average (blending heavy gazers and light gazers, seasonal variation, etc.)

## Calculation

$$200{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 10 \;\text{min/day} \times 365 \;\text{days} = 730{,}000{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-minutes}$$

$$\frac{730{,}000{,}000{,}000}{1{,}440 \;\text{min/day}} \approx 507{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

## My Point Estimate

$$\boxed{500{,}000{,}000 \text{ person-days}}$$

That is, roughly **500 million person-days** were spent looking through a window across all of humanity in the year 1800.

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

### World Population in 1800
~**1 billion** people.

### Fraction with Access to Transparent (Glass) Windows
Glass windows were widespread in Western/Central Europe (including most English cottages by 1800), but rare in Asia, Africa, and much of the rest of the world:

| Region | Population | % with glass windows | Count |
|---|---|---|---|
| Western/Central Europe | ~170M | ~50% | ~85M |
| Eastern Europe/Russia | ~80M | ~15% | ~12M |
| North America | ~7M | ~55% | ~4M |
| Latin America, Middle East, India, China, etc. | ~740M | ~1–3% | ~12M |

**Total with glass window access ≈ 115 million people**

### Average Daily Window-Gazing Time
Most people (>80%) were agricultural workers spending the bulk of daylight **outdoors**. Indoor waking time was limited (meals, evenings). Weighted across farmers (~3 min/day), urban workers (~7 min), the wealthy (~15 min), and children/elderly (~7 min):

**Average ≈ 5 minutes/day** per person with window access.

## Calculation

$$115{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 5 \;\text{min/day} \times 365 \;\text{days} = 2.10 \times 10^{11} \;\text{person-minutes}$$

$$\frac{2.10 \times 10^{11}}{1{,}440 \;\text{min/day}} \approx 145{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

## My Point Estimate

$$\boxed{150{,}000{,}000 \text{ person-days}}$$

That is, roughly **150 million person-days** of cumulative window-gazing across all of humanity in the year 1800.

# Estimating Total Window-Gazing Time in 1800

## Key Parameters

### World Population (1800): ~1 billion

### Fraction with Access to Windows (by region)

| Region | Population | % with windows | People with access |
|---|---|---|---|
| Europe & colonies | ~250M | ~60% | 150M |
| East Asia | ~350M | ~30% | 105M |
| South/Southeast Asia | ~250M | ~25% | 63M |
| Middle East/N. Africa | ~50M | ~35% | 18M |
| Sub-Saharan Africa | ~100M | ~10% | 10M |
| **Total** | | | **~345 million** |

### Average Daily Window-Gazing Time
Most people in 1800 were outdoor agricultural laborers with minimal indoor leisure. Even those indoors (women doing chores, elderly, urban dwellers) only glanced out occasionally — checking weather, watching for arrivals, brief idle gazing.

- Europe: ~6 min/day (more indoor culture, glass windows)
- East Asia: ~3 min/day (paper screens limit visibility)
- South/SE Asia: ~3 min/day
- Middle East: ~5 min/day
- Sub-Saharan Africa: ~2 min/day
- **Weighted average ≈ 4.4 min/day**

## Calculation

$$345{,}000{,}000 \times \frac{4.4}{1440} \times 365 \approx 384{,}000{,}000$$

## My Answer: **400,000,000 person-days**

(roughly 400 million days, or about 1.1 million person-years of cumulative window-gazing)

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population (1800):** ~1 billion

**Window access by region:**

| Region | Population | % with window access | Avg. gazing (min/day per capita) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Europe | 200M | ~70% | ~5 |
| China | 330M | ~50% (paper/open windows) | ~2 |
| Indian subcontinent | 200M | ~30% | ~2 |
| Americas (settlers) | 15M | ~70% | ~4 |
| Middle East | 30M | ~30% | ~3 |
| Japan/Korea | 35M | ~20% | ~2 |
| Africa / SE Asia / other | 160M | ~5–10% | ~1–2 |

## Reasoning on Gazing Duration
- ~80% of people were agricultural, spending most daylight hours **outdoors** → very little window-gazing (2–5 min/day)
- Urban/craft workers (~15%) were indoors more → ~15–20 min/day
- Wealthy/leisured (~5%) → ~20–30 min/day
- Nighttime window-gazing was negligible (no outdoor lighting)

## Calculation

Summing across regions gives roughly **1.5 billion person-minutes per day**.

Over a full year:
$$1.5 \times 10^9 \;\text{min/day} \times 365 \;\text{days} \div 1440 \;\text{min/day} \approx 380{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

## My Point Estimate

$$\boxed{400{,}000{,}000 \text{ person-days}}$$

# Estimating Total Person-Days Spent Looking Through a Window in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**Fraction with access to "look-through" windows:**
| Region | Population | % with windows | People with windows |
|--------|-----------|---------------|-------------------|
| Europe | ~200M | ~50% | ~100M |
| China/Japan/Korea | ~350M | ~25% (paper windows are translucent, but openable) | ~88M |
| South Asia | ~200M | ~20% | ~40M |
| Middle East | ~25M | ~35% | ~9M |
| Africa | ~100M | ~10% | ~10M |
| Americas | ~30M | ~20% | ~6M |
| **Total** | | | **~300M** |

**Average daily window-gazing time (~5 min/day):**
- Most people were outdoor agricultural laborers (very little indoor time during daylight)
- Indoor time was mostly after dark (windows useless for gazing)
- No modern indoor distractions, but also small, often poor-quality windows
- Weighted average across farmers (~3 min), urban workers (~7 min), wealthy/elderly/sick (~15–20 min)

## Calculation

$$300{,}000{,}000 \text{ people} \times 5 \text{ min/day} \times 365 \text{ days} = 547.5 \text{ billion person-minutes}$$

$$\frac{547{,}500{,}000{,}000}{1{,}440 \text{ min/day}} \approx 380{,}000{,}000 \text{ person-days}$$

## My Point Estimate

$$\boxed{380{,}000{,}000 \text{ person-days}}$$

That is, roughly **380 million person-days** were collectively spent looking through a window in the year 1800.
